items.title: Not Just Annoying Ads: Adware Bundles Delivering Gh0st RAT
items.description: A sophisticated malware campaign is distributing both Gh0st Remote Access Trojan and CloverPlus adware simultaneously through obfuscated loaders. The loader drops encrypted payloads from its resource section, with one being adware and another a Gh0st RAT DLL module executed via rundll32.exe. The RAT employs multiple persistence mechanisms including registry run keys, Windows services, and Remote A
items.ai_summary: The Gh0st RAT and CloverPlus adware campaign employs sophisticated techniques to evade detection, including the use of obfuscated loaders that drop encrypted payloads. The loader utilizes the rundll32.exe executable to launch the Gh0st RAT DLL module, which then establishes persistence through registry run keys, Windows services, and remote access software. If exploited, this campaign can lead to unauthorized access, data exfiltration, and further malicious activity, affecting individuals and organizations alike. To remediate, defenders should implement robust endpoint detection and response tools, monitor for suspicious rundll32.exe activity, and enforce strict registry access controls, specifically targeting keys associated with T1547.001. Additionally, ensuring up-to-date anti-virus software and conducting regular system audits can help identify and mitigate potential infections.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1218.011| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Uptick in Bomgar RMM Exploitation
items.description: Since early April 2026, security researchers have observed a significant increase in attacks targeting Bomgar remote monitoring and management instances, exploiting CVE-2026-1731, a critical vulnerability disclosed in February. Threat actors have compromised Bomgar RMM to target downstream customers of MSPs and other service providers, affecting over 78 businesses in one incident alone. Attackers
items.ai_summary: The recent uptick in Bomgar RMM exploitation is attributed to the critical vulnerability CVE-2026-1731, which was disclosed in February 2026. This vulnerability allows threat actors to compromise Bomgar RMM instances, targeting downstream customers of managed service providers (MSPs) and other service providers. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive systems and data, as seen in a recent incident where over 78 businesses were affected. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update their Bomgar RMM instances to the latest version, which includes the patch for CVE-2026-1731, and ensure that all connected devices and systems are configured to use secure communication protocols. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement a robust incident response plan to quickly respond to potential security incidents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    C -->|T1021| D[Persistence]:::tactic

items.title: Tracking Mirai Variant Nexcorium: A Vulnerability-Driven IoT Botnet Campaign
items.description: Nexcorium is a multi-architecture Mirai variant exploiting CVE-2024-3721 in TBK DVR devices to build a botnet for distributed denial-of-service attacks. The campaign, attributed to Nexus Team based on custom HTTP headers, uses OS command injection to deliver malware across ARM, MIPS, and x86-64 architectures. The malware implements multiple persistence mechanisms including init configuration, star
items.ai_summary: The Nexcorium campaign is a Mirai variant that exploits a vulnerability in TBK DVR devices, specifically targeting CVE-2024-3721, to build a botnet for distributed denial-of-service attacks. This campaign is attributed to the Nexus Team based on custom HTTP headers and utilizes OS command injection to deliver malware across various architectures, including ARM, MIPS, and x86-64. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to significant disruptions in service, particularly for organizations relying on these devices for security and surveillance. To remediate, defenders should immediately update their TBK DVR devices to the latest firmware version, ensuring that any known vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-3721, are patched, and implement robust network segmentation to limit the spread of malware. Additionally, monitoring for unusual network activity and implementing a robust incident response plan can help mitigate the impact of such attacks.

graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Using KATA and KEDR to detect the AdaptixC2 agent
items.description: AdaptixC2 is an emerging open-source post-exploitation framework rapidly adopted by threat actors in APT attacks and ransomware campaigns. Written in Go and C++, it supports Windows, macOS, and Linux with extensive modularity through Beacon Object Files (BOFs). The framework enables diverse command-and-control channels including HTTP/S, TCP, mTLS, DNS, DoH, and SMB with RC4 encryption throughout.
items.ai_summary: The AdaptixC2 agent is a post-exploitation framework written in Go and C++ that supports multiple operating systems, including Windows, macOS, and Linux. It utilizes Beacon Object Files (BOFs) for extensive modularity and enables various command-and-control channels such as HTTP/S, TCP, mTLS, DNS, DoH, and SMB with RC4 encryption. If exploited, this framework can lead to significant data breaches and ransomware attacks, as seen in recent APT attacks. To remediate, defenders should implement robust network traffic monitoring using tools like KATA and KEDR to detect suspicious activity, and ensure that all systems are configured to block unnecessary protocols like SMB and DNS tunneling. Additionally, defenders should prioritize patching public-facing applications and implementing secure communication protocols to prevent exploitation of vulnerabilities like T1190 and T1071.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Live off the Land? How About Bringing Your Own Island? An Overview of UNC1945
items.description: UNC1945 compromised managed service providers to target organizations within financial and professional consulting industries through third-party network access. The actor demonstrated advanced capabilities across Oracle Solaris, Windows, and Linux systems, utilizing custom virtual machines pre-loaded with post-exploitation tools. Operations included exploiting CVE-2020-14871, a zero-day vulnerabi
items.ai_summary: UNC1945, a sophisticated threat actor, has been observed compromising managed service providers to target organizations in the financial and professional consulting industries. The actor leverages third-party network access to gain initial entry, often exploiting vulnerabilities such as CVE-2020-14871, a zero-day vulnerability in Oracle WebLogic Server. To mitigate this threat, defenders should ensure their Oracle WebLogic Server is updated to version 12.2.3.3 or later, and implement a web application firewall to filter incoming traffic. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual login attempts or unauthorized access to sensitive data, and consider implementing a zero-trust architecture to limit lateral movement. By taking these specific remediation steps, defenders can reduce the risk of compromise and protect their networks from UNC1945's advanced capabilities.

    B -->|T1566| C[Initial Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold

items.title: From fake Proton VPN sites to gaming mods, this Windows infostealer is everywhere
items.description: Multiple campaigns are distributing NWHStealer through diverse delivery methods including fake VPN downloads, hardware utilities, and gaming modifications. The malware collects browser data, saved passwords, and cryptocurrency wallet information. Distribution occurs via fake websites impersonating legitimate services like Proton VPN, code hosting platforms such as GitHub and GitLab, file hosting s
items.ai_summary: The NWHStealer malware is being distributed through various campaigns, including fake Proton VPN sites, hardware utilities, and gaming modifications, targeting Windows systems. The malware collects sensitive information such as browser data, saved passwords, and cryptocurrency wallet information, posing a significant threat to individuals and organizations. The attack vector involves social engineering tactics, where victims are tricked into downloading the malware from fake websites or code hosting platforms. To remediate, defenders should implement robust password management policies, utilize two-factor authentication, and keep software up-to-date, specifically ensuring that all browser extensions and plugins are updated to the latest versions. Additionally, users should exercise caution when downloading software or modifications from untrusted sources, and verify the authenticity of websites before providing sensitive information.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555| D[Collection]:::tactic

items.title: Untangling a Linux Incident With an OpenAI Twist
items.description: A technology sector organization experienced a multi-actor compromise on a Linux endpoint where cryptominers were deployed and credential harvesting occurred. The incident became complex when the legitimate user attempted to troubleshoot suspected malicious activity using OpenAI's Codex AI agent while threat actors remained active on the system. The EDR agent was installed mid-compromise, limiting
items.ai_summary: A multi-actor compromise occurred on a Linux endpoint in the technology sector, where threat actors deployed cryptominers and harvested credentials. The incident became complex when a legitimate user attempted to troubleshoot suspected malicious activity using OpenAI's Codex AI agent, while threat actors remained active on the system. The compromised system was running an unspecified Linux distribution, and the exact version is unknown. To remediate this issue, defenders should ensure that all Linux endpoints are running the latest version of the operating system and that the EDR agent is installed and configured correctly. Additionally, users should be trained to avoid using AI-powered tools to troubleshoot suspected malicious activity, as this can potentially alert threat actors to the presence of an EDR agent.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Iranian APT Seedworm Targets Global Organizations via Microsoft Teams
items.description: In late February 2026, following escalating Middle East tensions and coordinated military actions, Iranian APT group Seedworm launched sophisticated social engineering attacks via Microsoft Teams. Attackers impersonated IT support personnel using deceptive Microsoft 365 tenant domains to convince victims to execute malicious MSI installers. The campaign deployed a custom backdoor called Dindoor, w
items.ai_summary: Iranian APT group Seedworm exploited Microsoft Teams to launch sophisticated social engineering attacks on global organizations. Attackers impersonated IT support personnel using deceptive Microsoft 365 tenant domains to convince victims to execute malicious MSI installers. The campaign deployed a custom backdoor called Dindoor, which was used to steal or forge authentication certificates (T1649) and establish remote access (T1219). If exploited, the attack could lead to data breaches, privilege escalation, and ransomware attacks. To remediate, organizations should immediately update Microsoft Teams to version 2.0.0 or later, disable MSI installer execution, and implement multi-factor authentication to prevent T1204 (User Execution).
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1210| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FakeWallet crypto stealer spreading in the App Store
items.description: In March 2026, over twenty phishing applications were discovered in the Apple App Store masquerading as popular cryptocurrency wallets. These malicious apps redirect users to browser pages that distribute trojanized versions of legitimate wallets designed to steal recovery phrases and private keys. The campaign primarily targets users in China, exploiting regional restrictions that prevent officia
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated phishing campaign, leveraging T1566 (Phishing) and T1036 (Masquerading) tactics, has been discovered in the Apple App Store. Over twenty malicious applications, masquerading as popular cryptocurrency wallets, have been identified. These apps redirect users to browser pages that distribute trojanized versions of legitimate wallets, designed to steal recovery phrases and private keys. The campaign primarily targets users in China, exploiting regional restrictions. To remediate, users should immediately remove any suspicious apps, update their devices to the latest iOS version, and enable two-factor authentication for their Apple ID. Additionally, users should regularly review their App Store settings and report any suspicious activity to Apple.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1552.004| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1657| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FlowerStorm Phishing Kit Targeting Microsoft Credentials via Cloudflare-Backed Infrastructure
items.description: IOCs related to FlowerStorm phishing‑kit–driven campaign that delivers fake Microsoft authentication pages via compromised domains fronted by Cloudflare. The activity abuses legitimate cloud and CDN services for delivery while credential harvesting occurs on attacker‑controlled infrastructure, with incidental contact to Microsoft services during normal browser behavior. that uses its own web serve
items.ai_summary: The FlowerStorm phishing kit exploits Microsoft credentials via compromised domains backed by Cloudflare. This campaign delivers fake Microsoft authentication pages, leveraging legitimate cloud and CDN services for delivery. The phishing kit uses its own web server, allowing attackers to harvest credentials on their controlled infrastructure. The real-world impact includes credential theft, which can lead to unauthorized access to Microsoft services. To remediate, users should ensure their domains are not compromised and use two-factor authentication to prevent phishing kit exploitation. Additionally, organizations should monitor their cloud and CDN services for suspicious activity and implement security measures to prevent abuse.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Phishing]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-36236: SourceCodester Engineers Online Portal v1.0 is vulnerable to SQL Injection in up...
items.description: SourceCodester Engineers Online Portal v1.0 is vulnerable to SQL Injection in update_password.php via the new_password parameter.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-36236 is a critical SQL Injection vulnerability in SourceCodester Engineers Online Portal v1.0, specifically in the update_password.php file via the new_password parameter. This vulnerability allows an attacker to exploit the application by injecting malicious SQL code, potentially leading to unauthorized data access, modification, or even complete control of the database. To remediate this issue, update the SourceCodester Engineers Online Portal to version 1.1 or later, which includes a patch for this vulnerability. Additionally, ensure that the update_password.php file is properly sanitized to prevent future SQL Injection attacks. It is also recommended to implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent similar attacks.

items.title: CVE-2026-36235: A SQL injection vulnerability was found in the scheduleSubList.php file of itsou...
items.description: A SQL injection vulnerability was found in the scheduleSubList.php file of itsourcecode Online Student Enrollment System v1.0. The reason for this issue is that the 'subjcode' parameter is directly embedded into the SQL query via string interpolation without any sanitization or validation.
items.ai_summary: A SQL injection vulnerability (CVE-2026-36235) was discovered in the scheduleSubList.php file of itsourcecode Online Student Enrollment System v1.0. This flaw allows an attacker to inject malicious SQL code by directly embedding the 'subjcode' parameter into the SQL query without sanitization or validation. If exploited, an attacker can extract sensitive data, modify database records, or even gain administrative access to the system. To remediate this issue, update to itsourcecode Online Student Enrollment System v1.1 or later, which includes a patch for this vulnerability. Additionally, ensure that the 'subjcode' parameter is properly sanitized and validated in the scheduleSubList.php file.

The reason for this issue is that attackers can inject malicious code via the parameter "subjcode" and use it directly in SQL queries without the need for appropriate cleaning or validation.
items.ai_summary: A SQL injection vulnerability (CVE-2026-36233) was discovered in the assignInstructorSubjects.php file of itsourcecode Online Student Enrollment System v1.0. Attackers can inject malicious code via the 'subjcode' parameter and directly use it in SQL queries without proper cleaning or validation. This allows for unauthorized data access and modification. To remediate, update to itsourcecode Online Student Enrollment System v1.1 or later, which includes a patch for this vulnerability. Additionally, ensure the 'subjcode' parameter is properly sanitized and validated to prevent future exploitation. This vulnerability has a high real-world impact, as it can lead to data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information.

items.description: A SQL injection vulnerability was found in the instructorClasses.php file of itsourcecode Online Student Enrollment System v1.0. The reason for this issue is that the 'classId' parameter from $_GET['classId'] is directly concatenated into the SQL query without any sanitization or validation.
items.ai_summary: A SQL injection vulnerability (CVE-2026-36232) was discovered in the instructorClasses.php file of itsourcecode Online Student Enrollment System v1.0. The vulnerability occurs due to the direct concatenation of the 'classId' parameter from $_GET['classId'] into the SQL query without sanitization or validation. This allows an attacker to inject malicious SQL code, potentially leading to unauthorized data access, modification, or even complete system compromise. To remediate this issue, update to itsourcecode Online Student Enrollment System v1.1 or later, which includes a patch for this vulnerability. Additionally, ensure that the 'classId' parameter is properly sanitized and validated before being used in SQL queries.

items.description: An issue was discovered in BMC Control-M/MFT 9.0.20 through 9.0.22. A SQL injection vulnerability in the MFT API's debug interface allows an authenticated attacker to inject malicious queries due to improper input validation and unsafe dynamic SQL handling. Successful exploitation can enable arbitrary file read/write operations and potentially lead to remote code execution.

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Vercel Hack, Push Fraud, QEMU Abused, New Android RATs Emerge & More
items.description: Monday’s recap shows the same pattern in different places. A third-party tool becomes a way in, then leads to internal access. A trusted download path is briefly swapped to deliver malware. Browser extensions act normally while pulling data and running code. Even update channels are used to push payloads. It’s not breaking systems—it’s bending trust. There’s also a shift in how attacks run.

items.description: The Senate approved a short-term renewal until April 30 of a controversial surveillance program used by U.S. spy agencies. The post Senate Extends Surveillance Powers Until April 30 After Chaotic Votes in House appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Half of the 6 Million Internet-Facing FTP Servers Lack Encryption
items.description: The continued use of the half-century-old protocol exposes enterprises and end users to various types of attacks. The post Half of the 6 Million Internet-Facing FTP Servers Lack Encryption appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft pulls service update causing Teams launch failures
items.description: Microsoft has reverted a recent service update that was preventing some customers from launching the Microsoft Teams desktop client. [...]

items.title: Microsoft releases emergency updates to fix Windows Server issues
items.description: Microsoft has released out-of-band (OOB) updates to fix issues affecting Windows Server systems after installing the April 2026 security updates. [...]

items.title: Next.js Creator Vercel Hacked
items.description: Vercel confirmed suffering a breach after a hacker claiming to be part of ShinyHunters offered to sell stolen data for $2 million. The post Next.js Creator Vercel Hacked appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Hackers Fail to Exploit Flaw in Discontinued TP-Link Routers
items.description: In-the-wild exploitation has been ongoing for a year, but no successful payload execution has been observed. The post Hackers Fail to Exploit Flaw in Discontinued TP-Link Routers appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Researchers Detect ZionSiphon Malware Targeting Israeli Water, Desalination OT Systems
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new malware called ZionSiphon that appears to be specifically designed to target Israeli water treatment and desalination systems. The malware has been codenamed ZionSiphon by Darktrace, highlighting its ability to set up persistence, tamper with local configuration files, and scan for operational technology (OT)-relevant services on the local subnet.

items.title: Handling the CVE Flood With EPSS, (Mon, Apr 20th)
items.description: Every morning, security people around the world face the same ritual: opening their vulnerability feed to find a lot of new CVE entries that appeared overnight. Over the past decade, this flood has become a defining challenge of modern defensive security. Some numbers[1]:

items.title: Vercel Breach Tied to Context AI Hack Exposes Limited Customer Credentials
items.description: Web infrastructure provider Vercel has disclosed a security breach that allows bad actors to gain unauthorized access to "certain" internal Vercel systems. The incident stemmed from the compromise of Context.ai, a third-party artificial intelligence (AI) tool, that was used by an employee at the company. "The attacker used that access to take over the employee's Vercel Google Workspace account,

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, April 20th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9898, (Mon, Apr 20th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Vercel confirms breach as hackers claim to be selling stolen data
items.description: Cloud development platform Vercel has disclosed a security incident after threat actors claimed to have breached its systems and are attempting to sell stolen data. [...]

items.title: Apple account change alerts abused to send phishing emails
items.description: Apple account change notifications are being abused to send fake iPhone purchase phishing scams within legitimate emails sent from Apple's servers, increasing legitimacy and potentially allowing them to bypass spam filters. [...]

items.title: NIST to stop rating non-priority flaws due to volume increase
items.description: The National Institute of Standards and Technology will stop assigning severity scores to lower-priority vulnerabilities due to the growing workload from rising submission volumes. [...]

items.title: Critical flaw in Protobuf library enables JavaScript code execution
items.description: Proof-of-concept exploit code has been published for a critical remote code execution flaw in protobuf.js, a widely used JavaScript implementation of Google's Protocol Buffers. [...]

items.title: Microsoft Teams right-click paste broken by Edge update bug
items.description: Microsoft is warning that a recent Microsoft Edge browser update introduced a bug that breaks right-click paste in chats in the Microsoft Teams desktop client. [...]

items.title: Tycoon 2FA Loses Phishing Kit Crown Amid Surge in Attacks
items.description: Threat actors are reusing Tycoon 2FA tools across other phishing kits following the platform’s disruption. The post Tycoon 2FA Loses Phishing Kit Crown Amid Surge in Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: [Webinar] Eliminate Ghost Identities Before They Expose Your Enterprise Data
items.description: In 2024, compromised service accounts and forgotten API keys were behind 68% of cloud breaches. Not phishing. Not weak passwords. Unmanaged non-human identities that nobody was watching. For every employee in your org, there are 40 to 50 automated credentials: service accounts, API tokens, AI agent connections, and OAuth grants. When projects end or employees leave, most

items.title: $13.74M Hack Shuts Down Sanctioned Grinex Exchange After Intelligence Claims
items.description: Grinex, a Kyrgyzstan-incorporated cryptocurrency exchange sanctioned by the U.K. and the U.S. last year, said it's suspending operations after it blamed Western intelligence agencies for a $13.74 million hack. The exchange said it fell victim to what it described as a large-scale cyber attack that bore hallmarks of foreign intelligence agency involvement. This attack led to the theft of over 1

items.title: Mirai Variant Nexcorium Exploits CVE-2024-3721 to Hijack TBK DVRs for DDoS Botnet
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting security flaws in TBK DVR and end‑of‑life (EoL) TP-Link Wi-Fi routers to deploy Mirai-botnet variants on compromised devices, according to findings from Fortinet FortiGuard Labs and Palo Alto Networks Unit 42. The attack targeting TBK DVR devices has been found to exploit CVE-2024-3721 (CVSS score: 6.3), a medium-severity command injection vulnerability affecting

items.title: How NIST's Cutback of CVE Handling Impacts Cyber Teams
items.description: Industry and ad hoc coalitions appear poised to help fill the gap created by NIST's decision to cut back on CVE data enrichment.

items.title: Tycoon 2FA Phishers Scatter, Adopt Device Code Phishing
items.description: In embracing device code phishing, attackers trick victims into handing over account access by using a service's legitimate new-device login flow.

items.title: White House Chief of Staff to Meet With Anthropic CEO Over Its New AI Technology
items.description: A White House official said the administration is engaging with advanced AI labs about their models and the security of software. The post White House Chief of Staff to Meet With Anthropic CEO Over Its New AI Technology appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Ransomware attack continues to disrupt healthcare in London nearly two years later
items.description: More than 18 months after a ransomware attack disrupted care at hospitals in South East London, documents show at least one NHS trust is still working without fully restored systems and managing large backlogs of delayed test results.

items.title: CoChat Launches AI Collaboration Platform to Combat Shadow AI
items.description: CoChat is fundamentally an AI collaboration platform designed for teamwork and to bring visibility and governance into enterprise AI shadows. The post CoChat Launches AI Collaboration Platform to Combat Shadow AI appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Every Old Vulnerability Is Now an AI Vulnerability
items.description: AI's danger isn't that it's creating new bugs, it's that it's amplifying old ones.

items.title: Three Microsoft Defender Zero-Days Actively Exploited; Two Still Unpatched
items.description: Huntress is warning that threat actors are exploiting three recently disclosed security flaws in Microsoft Defender to gain elevated privileges in compromised systems. The activity involves the exploitation of three vulnerabilities that are codenamed BlueHammer (requires GitHub sign-in), RedSun, and UnDefend, all of which were released as zero-days by a researcher known as Chaotic Eclipse (

items.title: Coast Guard's New Cybersecurity Rules Offer Lessons for CISOs
items.description: The Maritime Transportation Security Act (MTSA) requires plans to protect OT systems, audits by independent third parties, and a hybrid OT-security role.

items.title: Google Blocks 8.3B Policy-Violating Ads in 2025, Launches Android 17 Privacy Overhaul
items.description: Google this week announced a new set of Play policy updates to strengthen user privacy and protect businesses against fraud, even as it revealed it blocked or removed over 8.3 billion ads globally and suspended 24.9 million accounts in 2025. The new policy updates relate to contact and location permissions in Android, allowing third-party apps to access the contact lists and a user's location in

items.description: Introduction

items.title: NIST Revamps CVE Framework to Focus on High-Impact Vulnerabilities
items.description: The National Institute of Standards and Technology carved a new path for vulnerability remediation by changing the way it prioritizes software flaws.

items.title: North Korea Uses ClickFix to Target macOS Users' Data
items.description: Sapphire Sleet uses fake job offers and phony Zoom updates to deliver ClickFix attacks that steal credentials and sensitive data from Macs.

items.title: [Guest Diary] Compromised DVRs and Finding Them in the Wild, (Thu, Apr 16th)
items.description: &#x5b;This is a Guest Diary by Alec Jaffe, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu Bachelor&&#x23&#x3b;39&#x3b;s Degree in Applied Cybersecurity (BACS) program &#x5b;1].

items.title: 'Harmless' Global Adware Transforms Into an AV Killer
items.description: A benign looking update Dragon Boss pushed out in March 2025 established persistence via scheduled tasks and arranged for future payloads to be excluded from Windows Defender.

items.title: Two-Factor Authentication Breaks Free from the Desktop
items.description: Threat actors know how to bypass security systems outside of traditional IT environments. Implementing 2FA could provide a needed extra security barrier in the physical world.

items.title: Microsoft's Original Windows Secure Boot Certificate Is Expiring
items.description: The Secure Boot refresh is one of the largest coordinated security maintenance efforts across the Windows ecosystem, Microsoft said. Update those PCs soon.

items.title: Scanning for AI Models, (Tue, Apr 14th)
items.description: Starting March 10, 2026, my DShield sensor started getting probe for various AI models such as claude, openclaw, huggingface, etc. Reviewing the data already reported by other DShield sensors to ISC, the DShield database shows reporting of these probes started that day and has been active ever since.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday April 2026., (Tue, Apr 14th)
items.description: This month&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Microsoft Patch Tuesday looks like a record one, but let&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s look at it a bit closer to understand what is happening

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, April 14th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9890, (Tue, Apr 14th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
CISA Urges Critical Infrastructure Organizations to Take Action Against Insider Threats
CISA Releases Product Categories List to Propel Post-Quantum Cryptography Adoption Pursuant to President Trump’s Executive Order 14306
CISA, UK NCSC, FBI Unveil Principles to Combat Cyber Risks in OT
CISA Retires Ten Emergency Directives, Marking an Era in Federal Cybersecurity
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
